content.text: Red-team testing only protects production when it runs

— not just before launch. A structured cadence layering continuous automation, weekly sweeps, monthly campaigns, and periodic full exercises gives teams early warning, evidence for compliance, and a forcing function for remediation. The reporting artefacts turn raw findings into actionable risk data.
The red team's job is to find problems before attackers do. The cadence determines whether findings arrive in time to matter.
This sub-subtopic covers how to design a sustainable adversarial testing schedule, instrument the automated layers, produce structured finding reports, and track remediation through a risk register.

attrs.alt: Adversarial testing cadence and reporting pipeline

content.text: Different attack surfaces drift at different rates. Prompt-injection attempts adapt weekly; model behaviour drifts with each update; new features introduce fresh surfaces. A single annual penetration test cannot capture this dynamism. Instead, layer four cadence bands:
Replay previous critical probes against live endpoint
Manual campaign on current top attack surfaces
Continuous Layer: Probe Replay in CI
The cheapest finding is one caught in CI before a commit merges. Store a curated set of critical and high-severity probes as fixtures, then replay them on every deployment.

# tests/red_team/test_probe_replay.py
import pytest
from anthropic import Anthropic
from tests.red_team.fixtures import CRITICAL_PROBES  # versioned JSON list

client = Anthropic()

@pytest.mark.parametrize("probe", CRITICAL_PROBES)
def test_probe_does_not_regress(probe):
    """Replay historical red-team probes; fail if previously-safe behaviour reappears."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=probe["system_prompt"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": probe["user_input"]}],
    )
    text = response.content[0].text

    for forbidden in probe["forbidden_patterns"]:
        assert forbidden.lower() not in text.lower(), (
            f"Regression: probe '{probe['id']}' triggered forbidden pattern '{forbidden}'"
        )
Keep CRITICAL_PROBES in version control. Add every new critical/high finding from any cadence band to this file so the suite grows over time.

# tests/red_team/fixtures.py
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List

@dataclass
class RedTeamProbe:
    id: str                        # unique probe ID, e.g. "RT-2024-012"
    attack_surface: str            # e.g. "prompt_injection"
    severity: str                  # "critical" | "high" | "medium" | "low"
    system_prompt: str             # the system prompt under test
    user_input: str                # the adversarial user message
    forbidden_patterns: List[str]  # strings that must NOT appear in response
    rationale: str                 # why this probe matters
    mitre_ref: str = ""           # e.g. "AML.T0051.000"

# Load from JSON for easy editing without touching Python
import json, pathlib
_FIXTURE_PATH = pathlib.Path(__file__).parent / "probe_library.json"
CRITICAL_PROBES = [
    p for p in json.loads(_FIXTURE_PATH.read_text())
    if p["severity"] in ("critical", "high")
]
Weekly Sweep: Automated Scenario Library Execution

Once a week, run every scenario in the library — including medium and low severity probes — against staging. This sweeps for newly introduced vulnerabilities that wouldn't appear in the targeted CI set.

# scripts/red_team_weekly_sweep.py
"""Run full red-team library; write structured finding report."""
import json, datetime, pathlib
from anthropic import Anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import List

client = Anthropic()

@dataclass
class Finding:
    probe_id: str
    severity: str
    attack_surface: str
    triggered_pattern: str
    model_response_excerpt: str  # first 300 chars
    timestamp: str

def run_sweep(library_path: str) -> List[Finding]:
    library = json.loads(pathlib.Path(library_path).read_text())
    findings: List[Finding] = []

    for probe in library:
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=512,
            system=probe["system_prompt"],
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": probe["user_input"]}],
        )
        text = resp.content[0].text

        for pattern in probe["forbidden_patterns"]:
            if pattern.lower() in text.lower():
                findings.append(Finding(
                    probe_id=probe["id"],
                    severity=probe["severity"],
                    attack_surface=probe["attack_surface"],
                    triggered_pattern=pattern,
                    model_response_excerpt=text[:300],
                    timestamp=datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
                ))
                break  # one finding per probe

    return findings

def write_report(findings: List[Finding], out_path: str):
    report = {
        "run_date": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        "total_probes_run": None,  # filled in caller
        "total_findings": len(findings),
        "findings_by_severity": {
            sev: sum(1 for f in findings if f.severity == sev)
            for sev in ("critical", "high", "medium", "low")
        },
        "findings": [asdict(f) for f in findings],
    }
    pathlib.Path(out_path).write_text(json.dumps(report, indent=2))
    return report

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    library_path = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "tests/red_team/probe_library.json"
    out_path = f"reports/weekly_sweep_{datetime.date.today()}.json"
    pathlib.Path("reports").mkdir(exist_ok=True)

    findings = run_sweep(library_path)
    report = write_report(findings, out_path)
    print(f"Sweep complete: {report['total_findings']} findings → {out_path}")

Automated probes replay known patterns; human testers find novel ones. Monthly manual campaigns focus on a rotating surface (one month: tool misuse; next: multi-turn manipulation; next: data exfiltration). Structure the campaign as a time-boxed sprint:
Brief (30 min) — share current threat model, recent incident context, surface focus
Attack phase (3–6 h) — testers work in pairs, real-time Slack thread for coordination
Triage session (1 h) — classify every finding before the day ends
Draft report (next day) — PM/security lead writes executive summary
Remediation planning (sprint planning) — engineering assigns owners

# red_team/triage.py
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class Severity(Enum):
    CRITICAL = "critical"   # immediate data harm, safety violation, or privilege escalation
    HIGH = "high"           # significant harm possible; affects many users or core flows
    MEDIUM = "medium"       # limited harm; unusual inputs or low-probability paths
    LOW = "low"             # cosmetic or unlikely edge case

class Disposition(Enum):
    ACCEPTED = "accepted"           # confirmed, remediation planned
    REJECTED = "rejected"           # not a security issue; explain why
    WONT_FIX = "wont_fix"          # accepted risk, documented in register
    NEEDS_REPRO = "needs_repro"    # cannot reproduce; testers to retry with steps

@dataclass
class FindingRecord:
    id: str                          # e.g. "RT-2025-Q3-007"
    title: str
    severity: Severity
    attack_surface: str
    steps_to_reproduce: str
    observed_behaviour: str
    expected_behaviour: str
    disposition: Disposition
    owner: str                       # GitHub handle or team
    sla_deadline: str                # ISO date based on severity
    linked_probe_id: str = ""       # if a replay probe was created

def severity_sla(severity: Severity, campaign_date: str) -> str:
    """Return ISO deadline string based on severity SLA."""
    import datetime
    base = datetime.date.fromisoformat(campaign_date)
    delta = {
        Severity.CRITICAL: 1,
        Severity.HIGH: 14,
        Severity.MEDIUM: 60,
        Severity.LOW: 180,
    }[severity]
    return (base + datetime.timedelta(days=delta)).isoformat()

The quarterly exercise is a full-scope engagement: all attack surfaces, coordinated cross-functional team (engineers, security, product), and a formal deliverable reviewed by leadership. This cadence aligns with most enterprise risk frameworks.
Kick-off, threat model refresh, surface assignment
Attack phase — parallel teams per surface
Finding triage, severity assignment, PoC validation
Report writing, exec summary, risk register delta
Every red-team run — whether a CI check, weekly sweep, or quarterly exercise — should produce a consistent set of artefacts so findings can be trended over time and consumed by different audiences.

# red_team/reporting.py
"""Generate human-readable reports from finding JSON."""
import json, pathlib, datetime
from typing import List, Dict

def executive_summary(report: Dict, previous_report: Dict | None = None) -> str:
    findings = report.get("findings", [])
    by_sev: Dict[str, List] = {"critical": [], "high": [], "medium": [], "low": []}
    for f in findings:
        sev = f.get("severity", "low")
        if sev in by_sev:
            by_sev[sev].append(f)

    lines = [
        f"# Red-Team Report — {report.get('run_date', 'Unknown')}",
        "",
        "## Summary",
        f"- **Total probes run:** {report.get('total_probes', len(findings))}",
        f"- **Total findings:** {len(findings)}",
        f"  - Critical: {len(by_sev['critical'])}",
        f"  - High: {len(by_sev['high'])}",
        f"  - Medium: {len(by_sev['medium'])}",
        f"  - Low: {len(by_sev['low'])}",
    ]

    if previous_report:
        prev_count = len(previous_report.get("findings", []))
        delta = len(findings) - prev_count
        direction = "+" if delta >= 0 else ""
        lines.append(f"- **Delta vs previous run:** {direction}{delta}")

    lines += ["", "## Critical & High Findings"]
    for sev in ("critical", "high"):
        for f in by_sev[sev]:
            lines.append(f"- [{sev.upper()}] {f.get('probe_id','')} — {f.get('attack_surface','')}: {f.get('triggered_pattern','')}")

    if not by_sev["critical"] and not by_sev["high"]:
        lines.append("None — no critical or high findings this run.")

    lines += ["", "## Trend", "See trend_dashboard.json for SLA compliance and open count history."]
    return "
".join(lines)

def write_finding_csv(findings: List[Dict], out_path: str):
    import csv
    fields = ["probe_id", "severity", "attack_surface", "triggered_pattern", "timestamp"]
    with open(out_path, "w", newline="") as f:
        w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=fields, extrasaction="ignore")
        w.writeheader()
        w.writerows(findings)
Raw finding counts are less useful than trend signals. Build a lightweight JSON time series that captures open/closed counts and SLA compliance per cadence run:

# red_team/trend.py
"""Append a cadence run's metrics to a persistent trend JSON file."""
import json, pathlib, datetime
from typing import List, Dict

TREND_PATH = pathlib.Path("reports/trend_dashboard.json")

def append_run_metrics(report: Dict, sla_breaches: int):
    """Add one row per run to the trend time series."""
    if TREND_PATH.exists():
        data = json.loads(TREND_PATH.read_text())
    else:
        data = {"runs": []}

    run_metrics = {
        "date": report.get("run_date", datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat()),
        "cadence": report.get("cadence", "unknown"),
        "total_findings": report.get("total_findings", 0),
        "critical": report.get("findings_by_severity", {}).get("critical", 0),
        "high": report.get("findings_by_severity", {}).get("high", 0),
        "medium": report.get("findings_by_severity", {}).get("medium", 0),
        "low": report.get("findings_by_severity", {}).get("low", 0),
        "sla_breaches": sla_breaches,
    }
    data["runs"].append(run_metrics)
    TREND_PATH.write_text(json.dumps(data, indent=2))
    return data

def compute_sla_breaches(findings: List[Dict]) -> int:
    """Count findings that have passed their SLA deadline without closing."""
    today = datetime.date.today()
    breaches = 0
    for f in findings:
        sla = f.get("sla_deadline")
        if sla and f.get("status") != "closed":
            if datetime.date.fromisoformat(sla) < today:
                breaches += 1
    return breaches
Plug the weekly sweep into CI so regressions block merges to production. A separate workflow runs on a schedule rather than every commit:

# .github/workflows/red_team_weekly.yml
name: Weekly Red-Team Sweep
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 6 * * 1"   # every Monday 06:00 UTC
  workflow_dispatch:        # allow manual trigger

jobs:
  sweep:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install anthropic pytest

      - name: Run probe replay (critical + high only)
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: pytest tests/red_team/test_probe_replay.py -v --tb=short

      - name: Run full library sweep
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: python scripts/red_team_weekly_sweep.py tests/red_team/probe_library.json

      - name: Upload finding report
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: red-team-findings
          path: reports/weekly_sweep_*.json
          retention-days: 90
Finding a vulnerability and not fixing it on schedule is a process failure. Track SLA compliance explicitly:
Deploy a server-side guardrail or disable the affected feature immediately. Patch follows within 24 h.
An engineering ticket is created and accepted into the next sprint. No exceptions without documented waiver.
Added to product backlog with severity label. Reviewed at each quarterly exercise.
Recorded in risk register. Addressed during technical-debt sprints or when the affected area is next refactored.
Layer cadence bands: continuous CI replay → weekly automated sweep → monthly manual campaign → quarterly full exercise → annual external audit
Store probes as versioned fixtures in version control so the suite accumulates all confirmed findings
Produce structured JSON reports from every automated run; generate human-readable executive summaries for stakeholder consumption
Maintain a persistent risk register with open/closed status and SLA deadlines for every finding
Track trend metrics (counts, SLA breaches) across runs to detect directional risk changes
Severity-based SLAs — critical 24 h, high sprint+1, medium 60 days, low 180 days — turn findings into enforced commitments
CI gates (probe replay on every push; weekly sweep on schedule) prevent regressions from shipping to production
